title: Howie Roseman

(born June 23, 1975) is an American professional [football](/wiki/American_football "American football") executive who is the executive vice president and [general manager](/wiki/General_manager_(American_football) "General manager (American football)") for the [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles") of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL). He was hired by the team in 2000 and served in various administrative and executive roles before being promoted to general manager in 2010.[[1]](#cite_note-PE.com-1)

**Howard Roseman** (born June 23, 1975) is an American professional [football](/wiki/American_football "American football") executive who is the executive vice president and [general manager](/wiki/General_manager_(American_football) "General manager (American football)") for the [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles") of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL). He was hired by the team in 2000 and served in various administrative and executive roles before being promoted to general manager in 2010.[[1]](#cite_note-PE.com-1)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Howie_Roseman&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Roseman was born in [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"), [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") on June 23, 1975, and is [Jewish](/wiki/Jewish "Jewish").[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) He grew up in [Marlboro Township](/wiki/Marlboro_Township,_New_Jersey "Marlboro Township, New Jersey"), [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"),[[5]](#cite_note-5) and later graduated from [Marlboro High School](/wiki/Marlboro_High_School "Marlboro High School").[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-KYW2013-7)[[1]](#cite_note-PE.com-1)

Roseman began sending letters to every NFL team while he was still in high school. His persistence increased while he was an undergraduate at the [University of Florida](/wiki/University_of_Florida "University of Florida") and throughout his time at [Fordham University School of Law](/wiki/Fordham_University_School_of_Law "Fordham University School of Law"). His job inquiries led him to connect with [Mike Tannenbaum](/wiki/Mike_Tannenbaum "Mike Tannenbaum"), then the pro personnel director of the [New York Jets](/wiki/New_York_Jets "New York Jets"). Tannenbaum interviewed Roseman for a player personnel intern position in 1999, but he did not get the job.[[7]](#cite_note-KYW2013-7)

## Professional career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Howie_Roseman&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Professional career")]

After another pursuit, Roseman was hired by the [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles") as an intern to work on salary cap issues in [2000](/wiki/2000_NFL_season "2000 NFL season"). He was promoted to director of football administration in [2003](/wiki/2003_NFL_season "2003 NFL season") and was later promoted to vice president of football administration in [2006](/wiki/2006_NFL_season "2006 NFL season"). Roseman continued to climb the Eagles front office ladder, serving as the vice president of player personnel for two years before being named the Eagles [general manager](/wiki/General_manager "General manager") on January 29, 2010 after [Tom Heckert](/wiki/Tom_Heckert,_Jr. "Tom Heckert, Jr.") was hired by the [Cleveland Browns](/wiki/2010_Cleveland_Browns_season "2010 Cleveland Browns season") in the same role.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Although he had the title of general manager, Roseman served mainly in an advisory role to head coach and executive vice president of football operations [Andy Reid](/wiki/Andy_Reid "Andy Reid"), who had the final say in football matters.

Within his first season as general manager, Roseman went to work on the Eagles’ roster, building it into one of the youngest in the league. That revamped Eagles squad earned an NFC East division championship in 2010.[[1]](#cite_note-PE.com-1)

Three years later, Roseman assisted Eagles chairman Jeffrey Lurie in the team's search for a new head coach in 2013, which ended with University of Oregon coach [Chip Kelly](/wiki/Chip_Kelly "Chip Kelly") coming to Philadelphia.[[1]](#cite_note-PE.com-1) Kelly, like Reid, had the final say over the 53-man roster, so Roseman continued to serve mainly in an advisory role.

In their first season together in Philadelphia, Roseman and Kelly oversaw an Eagles team that won 10 games and a division championship, in a considerable turnaround from the team's 4–12 record in 2012.

Among Roseman's responsibilities as the general manager, he oversaw the Eagles’ college and pro scouting departments, the team's medical, equipment, and video staffs, while also controlling the team's salary cap and supervising team security.[[1]](#cite_note-PE.com-1)

In a change of front office structure, on January 2, 2015, Kelly was given general manager duties while Roseman was promoted to executive vice president of football operations. In this role, Roseman continued directing contract negotiations, managing the team's salary and also overseeing the team's medical staff, equipment staff and more.[[1]](#cite_note-PE.com-1) After Kelly's dismissal, Roseman became the de facto general manager again in 2016, and hired [Doug Pederson](/wiki/Doug_Pederson "Doug Pederson") as the Eagles' new head coach. Roseman helped the Eagles win [Super Bowl LII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LII "Super Bowl LII") when the team defeated the [New England Patriots](/wiki/New_England_Patriots "New England Patriots") 41–33 in 2018.[[9]](#cite_note-u266-9) He was promoted to executive vice president and general manager on June 13, 2019. He won a second Super Bowl championship when the Eagles defeated the Kansas City Chiefs 40–22 in [Super Bowl LIX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LIX "Super Bowl LIX").[[10]](#cite_note-a252-10)

Roseman also plays a large role in the Eagles' community efforts, contributing to a number of initiatives involving military and children. Roseman's charitable endeavors have benefited Eagles Youth Partnership, the team's public charity which serves over 50,000 low-income children in the Greater Philadelphia region every year with a focus on health and education programming, as well as Eagles Care. In addition, Roseman has also worked with [Alex's Lemonade Stand Foundation](/wiki/Alex%27s_Lemonade_Stand_Foundation "Alex's Lemonade Stand Foundation"), the [Boys & Girls Clubs of America](/wiki/Boys_%26_Girls_Clubs_of_America "Boys & Girls Clubs of America"), [USA Football](/wiki/USA_Football "USA Football"), [Pop Warner](/wiki/Pop_Warner_Little_Scholars "Pop Warner Little Scholars"), and Cop Wheels. He was named Honorary Commander for the [Joint Base McGuire-Dix-Lakehurst](/wiki/Joint_Base_McGuire-Dix-Lakehurst "Joint Base McGuire-Dix-Lakehurst") for his continued efforts with the military.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Musical career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Howie_Roseman&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Musical career")]

Roseman is a featured artist on "[The Dreidel Song](/wiki/The_Dreidel_Song "The Dreidel Song")" as covered by The Philly Specials – a vocal trio composed of [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles") offensive linemen [Lane Johnson](/wiki/Lane_Johnson "Lane Johnson"), [Jason Kelce](/wiki/Jason_Kelce "Jason Kelce"), and [Jordan Mailata](/wiki/Jordan_Mailata "Jordan Mailata") – on their second album, *[A Philly Special Christmas Special](/wiki/A_Philly_Special_Christmas_Special "A Philly Special Christmas Special")*.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Proceeds from the album were donated to the Children's Crisis Treatment Center and [Children's Hospital of Philadelphia](/wiki/Children%27s_Hospital_of_Philadelphia "Children's Hospital of Philadelphia").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Howie_Roseman&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Roseman earned his bachelor's degree from the [University of Florida](/wiki/University_of_Florida "University of Florida") before earning a JD degree from [Fordham Law School](/wiki/Fordham_Law_School "Fordham Law School"). He was roommates and fraternity brothers with [Jedd Fisch](/wiki/Jedd_Fisch "Jedd Fisch") during their time at Florida.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Howie and his wife, Mindy, reside in the Philadelphia suburbs with their four children.[[1]](#cite_note-PE.com-1)[[14]](#cite_note-14)
